reference: GATE CS — Digital Logic / Number Systems

map.condition: Converting / ranging a fixed-point n-bit number with f fraction bits
Finding Booth's recoding of a bit pair
Converting a number from base r to base 10

formulas.name: Positional (Weighted) Value of a Number
formulas.plain: N (base 10) = sum of d_i * r^i for each digit d_i in base r
formulas.whenToUse: Converting any base-r integer or fraction to decimal
formulas.trap: Remember that fraction digit positions have negative exponents (e.g., position -1 means r^{-1}); confusing the sign of i is a common error.

formulas.name: Range of an n-bit Unsigned Integer
formulas.plain: Range = 0 to 2^n - 1 (total 2^n distinct values)
formulas.whenToUse: Finding how many values an n-bit unsigned binary integer can represent
formulas.trap: For signed 2's complement the range is [-2^{n-1}, 2^{n-1}-1]; do not apply the unsigned formula to signed numbers.

formulas.name: Two's Complement Representation
formulas.plain: 2's complement of X = 2^n - X (invert all bits, then add 1)
formulas.whenToUse: Representing negative integers or computing subtraction via addition
formulas.trap: The most negative number (-2^{n-1}) has no positive counterpart in n bits; negating it overflows.

formulas.name: Repeated Division / Multiplication for Base Conversion
formulas.latex: \text{Integer part: repeated divide by } r \quad \text{Fraction part: repeated multiply by } r
formulas.plain: Integer: divide by r, collect remainders (LSB first). Fraction: multiply by r, collect integer parts (MSB first).
formulas.whenToUse: Converting a decimal number to any base r
formulas.trap: Fraction conversion may not terminate; carry enough bits to achieve required precision.

formulas.name: Signed-Magnitude vs 1's Complement vs 2's Complement Zero
formulas.plain: Signed-magnitude and 1's complement have two representations of zero; 2's complement has exactly one zero.
formulas.whenToUse: Comparing representation schemes and counting representable values
formulas.trap: In an n-bit 1's complement system there are 2^n - 1 distinct values (not 2^n) because of dual zero.

formulas.name: Unsigned Fixed-Point Range and Resolution
formulas.plain: Range: 0 to (2^i - 2^(-f)), resolution = 2^(-f), where i = integer bits = n - f
formulas.whenToUse: Finding the range and smallest representable step for an n-bit unsigned fixed-point number with f fraction bits

terms.means: fraction bits (bits to the right of binary point)

formulas.trap: The maximum value is NOT 2^i; it is 2^i - 2^{-f} (the resolution step is subtracted because you cannot reach 2^i exactly).

formulas.name: Decimal Value of a Fixed-Point Binary Pattern
formulas.plain: V = sum of each bit times its positional weight; bits left of point have positive powers, bits right have negative powers
formulas.whenToUse: Reading off the decimal value of a fixed-point binary string given the position of the binary point
formulas.trap: Always note where the binary point is placed; changing its position changes the value even with the same bit pattern.

formulas.name: General Floating-Point Value Formula
formulas.plain: V = (-1)^S * M * beta^E, where S is sign, M is mantissa, beta is base, E is exponent
formulas.whenToUse: Interpreting any floating-point format (binary, hex-based, BCD, etc.)

terms.means: base of the floating-point system (2, 16, 10, etc.)

formulas.trap: When the base is 16 (IBM hex float), one bit of precision is lost per leading hex zero in the mantissa — precision is worse than IEEE 754.

formulas.name: Biased (Excess-N) Exponent Encoding
formulas.plain: True exponent = stored exponent - bias; standard bias = 2^(e-1) - 1 where e is number of exponent bits
formulas.whenToUse: Decoding the true exponent from a biased stored value (IEEE 754 and similar formats)

terms.means: number of bits in the exponent field
unsigned integer value of exponent field
offset subtracted to get true exponent

formulas.trap: All-zeros and all-ones exponent fields are reserved (denormals, infinity, NaN in IEEE 754); do not apply the normal formula to them.

formulas.name: Normalization Condition
formulas.latex: \frac{1}{\beta} \leq M < 1 \quad (\text{or } 1 \leq M < \beta \text{ for IEEE hidden-bit style})
formulas.plain: For base-beta: 1/beta <= M < 1 (fraction form) or 1 <= M < beta (integer form with hidden 1)
formulas.whenToUse: Checking whether a floating-point number is in normalized form or determining adjustment needed
formulas.trap: For base-2 with a hidden bit (IEEE), the leading 1 is implicit — the stored fraction field does NOT include it; forgetting the hidden 1 gives a wrong value.

formulas.name: Floating-Point Exponent Range (Biased, e bits)
formulas.plain: True exponent range: -(2^(e-1) - 2) to 2^(e-1) - 1 (stored values 1 to 2^e - 2; 0 and 2^e-1 are reserved)
formulas.whenToUse: Determining the exponent range for a given number of exponent bits in IEEE-style format
formulas.trap: The stored exponent range for normal numbers is 1 to 2^e - 2 (not 0 to 2^e - 1), so the true exponent range is asymmetric.

formulas.name: Hex-Based Floating Point Exponent Range
formulas.latex: V = M \times 16^E, \quad E \in [-64, 63] \text{ (excess-64 encoding with 7-bit exponent)}
formulas.plain: Value = M * 16^E; with 7-bit exponent and bias 64, E ranges from -64 to +63
formulas.whenToUse: IBM-style or base-16 floating point where exponent base is 16 instead of 2
formulas.trap: Shifting the mantissa by 1 hex digit (4 bits) changes the exponent by 1; up to 3 leading zero bits may appear, wasting mantissa precision.

formulas.name: IEEE 754 Single-Precision Format (32-bit)
formulas.plain: Value = (-1)^S * 1.fraction * 2^(stored_exponent - 127); 1 sign bit, 8 exponent bits, 23 mantissa bits
formulas.whenToUse: Encoding or decoding a 32-bit IEEE 754 single-precision float
formulas.trap: The leading 1 before the binary point is implicit (hidden bit) and not stored; forgetting it makes the decoded value half of the correct answer.

formulas.name: IEEE 754 Double-Precision Format (64-bit)
formulas.plain: Value = (-1)^S * 1.fraction * 2^(stored_exponent - 1023); 1 sign, 11 exponent, 52 mantissa bits
formulas.whenToUse: Encoding or decoding a 64-bit IEEE 754 double-precision float
formulas.trap: Bias is 1023 for double, not 127; mixing up the two biases is a frequent GATE error.

formulas.name: IEEE 754 Special Values (Exponent = 0 or All-1s)
formulas.latex: \begin{cases} E=0, F=0 & \Rightarrow \pm 0 \\ E=0, F\neq 0 & \Rightarrow \text{Denormal (subnormal)} \\ E=255, F=0 & \Rightarrow \pm\infty \\ E=255, F\neq 0 & \Rightarrow \text{NaN} \end{cases}
formulas.plain: Exponent=0, Frac=0 -> +/-0; Exponent=0, Frac!=0 -> denormal; Exponent=255, Frac=0 -> +/-inf; Exponent=255, Frac!=0 -> NaN
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying special IEEE 754 values or checking if a bit pattern represents a normal number
formulas.trap: Denormals use 0.F (not 1.F) and exponent -126 (not -127); confusing the two leads to wrong decoded values.

formulas.name: Denormal (Subnormal) Number Value
formulas.plain: Denormal value = (-1)^S * 0.fraction * 2^(-126); note: no hidden 1, and exponent fixed at -126
formulas.whenToUse: Decoding a denormal IEEE 754 single-precision number (stored exponent = 0, fraction != 0)
formulas.trap: The effective exponent is -126, not -127; even though stored exponent = 0, the rule is designed to provide a smooth transition from normal to denormal.

formulas.name: Machine Epsilon and Precision
formulas.plain: Machine epsilon = 2^(-23) for single precision; 2^(-52) for double precision (smallest value such that 1 + eps != 1)
formulas.whenToUse: Estimating floating-point rounding error or the number of significant decimal digits
formulas.trap: Machine epsilon is NOT the smallest positive float; the smallest normal single is ~1.18e-38 and the smallest denormal is even smaller.

formulas.name: Booth Recoding Rule (Radix-2)
formulas.plain: Booth digit at position i = b_i minus b_{i+1} where b_{-1} = 0 (appended bit). Result is -1, 0, or +1.
formulas.whenToUse: Recoding a multiplier in Booth's algorithm to reduce partial products

terms.means: next higher multiplier bit (b_{-1}=0 for LSB)

formulas.trap: Always append a 0 bit below the LSB (b_{-1} = 0); omitting it gives wrong recoding for the least significant pair.

formulas.name: Booth Partial-Product Operations
formulas.plain: Booth digit +1 -> add multiplicand; -1 -> subtract multiplicand; 0 -> no operation (shift only)
formulas.whenToUse: Deciding which operation to perform at each step of Booth's multiplication

terms.means: no add or subtract, just shift

formulas.trap: Subtraction means adding the 2's complement of the multiplicand, NOT just flipping bits (must add 1 for 2's complement).

formulas.name: Booth's Algorithm Worst-Case Pattern
formulas.latex: \text{Worst case: alternating } 1010\ldots10 \Rightarrow \text{all non-zero digits, } \lceil n/2 \rceil \text{ adds/subs}
formulas.plain: Worst case multiplier pattern is alternating 1010...10 — every Booth digit is non-zero, giving maximum additions/subtractions
formulas.whenToUse: Determining when Booth's algorithm has worst performance (most partial products to accumulate)
formulas.trap: All-1s (0b1111...1) gives only ONE non-zero Booth digit (at the ends), so it is actually the BEST case, not worst — a common trap in GATE MCQs.

formulas.name: Number of Steps in Booth's Algorithm
formulas.plain: Booth's algorithm for n-bit multiplier requires exactly n steps (one per bit), each with an optional add/subtract followed by arithmetic right shift
formulas.whenToUse: Counting iterations/steps in a Booth multiplication implementation

terms.means: number of bits in the multiplier

formulas.trap: The product of two n-bit numbers is 2n bits wide; the accumulator register must be wide enough to hold 2n bits to avoid overflow.

formulas.name: Overflow Detection in 2's Complement Addition
formulas.plain: Overflow occurs when carry into the sign bit differs from carry out of the sign bit (XOR of the two carries = 1)
formulas.whenToUse: Detecting overflow after adding two signed 2's complement numbers

terms.means: carry into the most significant (sign) bit position
carry out of the most significant bit position

formulas.trap: Overflow can only occur when both operands have the same sign; it is impossible when adding numbers of opposite signs.

formulas.name: BCD Addition Correction
formulas.plain: BCD addition: if the 4-bit result > 9 or carry out = 1, add 6 (0110) to correct the result to valid BCD
formulas.whenToUse: Performing BCD (8421) arithmetic addition

terms.means: carry out of the 4-bit BCD digit

formulas.trap: The correction is adding 6 (decimal), not just setting a bit; forgetting the carry propagation from the correction into the next digit is also common.

formulas.name: Floating-Point Rounding Modes
formulas.plain: IEEE 754 rounding modes: round-to-nearest-even (default), truncate toward zero, round toward +inf, round toward -inf
formulas.whenToUse: Analyzing or implementing floating-point arithmetic results subject to rounding

terms.means: extra bit kept during intermediate computation to improve rounding accuracy

formulas.trap: Default IEEE rounding is round-to-nearest-even (banker's rounding), NOT always round-up; this affects tie-breaking.
